content:
# Ruth Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 881–882 | **Source Image:** RUT_003_page_001.jpg, RUT_003_page_002.jpg

---

**1** One day Naomi, her mother-in-law, said to her, "My daughter, shouldn't I find you a home where you will be provided for?

**2** Now listen — Boaz, whose young women you have been working alongside, is our relative. Tonight he will be winnowing barley at the threshing floor.

**3** So wash yourself, put on perfume, and dress in your best clothes. Then go down to the threshing floor. But don't let him know you are there until he has finished eating and drinking.

**4** When he lies down, take note of where he settles. Then go, uncover his feet, and lie down. He will tell you what to do."

**5** Ruth said to her, "I will do everything you say."

**6** So she went down to the threshing floor and did exactly as her mother-in-law had instructed.

**7** After Boaz had eaten and drunk and was in good spirits, he went to lie down at the far end of the grain pile. Then Ruth came quietly, uncovered his feet, and lay down.

**8** In the middle of the night, the man was startled. He rolled over — and there was a woman lying at his feet!

**9** "Who are you?" he asked. She answered, "I am Ruth, your servant. Spread your cloak over me, for you are a family redeemer."

**10** He said, "The LORD bless you, my daughter. This act of loyalty surpasses even your first — you have not gone after younger men, whether poor or rich.

**11** And now, my daughter, don't be afraid. I will do everything you ask. Everyone in town knows that you are a woman of noble character.

**12** Now it is true that I am a redeemer, but there is another man who is a closer relative than I am.

**13** Stay here tonight. In the morning, if he is willing to redeem you — good, let him redeem you. But if he is not willing, then as surely as the LORD lives, I will redeem you myself. Lie down until morning."

**14** So she lay at his feet until morning but got up before anyone could recognize another person. Boaz said, "No one must know that a woman came to the threshing floor."

**15** Then he said, "Bring the shawl you are wearing and hold it open." She held it out, and he poured six measures of barley into it. He placed it on her back, and she went into the town.

**16** When she came to her mother-in-law, Naomi asked, "How did it go, my daughter?" Ruth told her everything the man had done for her.

**17** She said, "He gave me these six measures of barley and told me, 'Don't go back to your mother-in-law empty-handed.'"

**18** Naomi said, "Sit tight, my daughter, until you see how this turns out. That man will not rest until he has settled this matter today."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "a home where you will be provided for":** The Hebrew מָנוֹחַ (manoach) means "rest" or "security" — specifically the settled security a woman found in marriage. Naomi is not merely matchmaking; she is seeking Ruth's future safety and well-being.

**Verse 3 — The preparation:** Washing, perfuming, and dressing in fine clothes echo bridal preparation. Naomi is staging a discreet proposal. The threshing floor at night was a liminal, charged space — the narrative trusts the reader to sense the tension.

**Verse 9 — "Spread your cloak over me":** Ruth's words וּפָרַשְׂתָּ כְנָפֶךָ (ufarasta kenafekha) literally mean "spread your wing over your servant." This echoes Boaz's own words in 2:12 about God's sheltering wings. Ruth boldly asks Boaz to become the human embodiment of the divine protection he invoked. The word כָּנָף means both "wing" and the corner of a garment — spreading one's garment over a woman was a gesture of marriage proposal.

**Verse 10 — "This act of loyalty surpasses even your first":** Boaz identifies two acts of חֶסֶד (hesed) from Ruth — the first was her devotion to Naomi; the second is her choosing a kinsman-redeemer rather than a younger man, thereby preserving the family line. She has put family obligation above personal desire.

**Verse 11 — "a woman of noble character":** The Hebrew אֵשֶׁת חַיִל (eshet chayil) — the same phrase that opens Proverbs 31. This is the highest compliment available in Israelite culture. Boaz was introduced as an אִישׁ גִּבּוֹר חַיִל (2:1); now Ruth is revealed as his match.

**Verse 15 — "six measures of barley":** The gift functions as a pledge to Naomi — a sign that Boaz intends to act. The word "empty" (רֵיקָם, reqam) in verse 17 echoes Naomi's bitter lament in 1:21 that the LORD brought her back "empty." Boaz is beginning to reverse Naomi's emptiness.

**Verse 18 — "Sit tight":** Naomi's counsel to wait reflects her confidence in Boaz's character. The Hebrew שְׁבִי (shevi) is simply "sit" or "remain still" — Naomi knows that restless action is no longer needed. The man will act.

— PNEUMA
